title: Here are five ways you could build a Slate EV, from $25,000 to $37,000
gist: Slate Auto's configurator reveals the electric truck can be built for $24,950 (bare-bones pickup) up to $36,819 for a fully optioned SUV, undercutting most electric crossovers. The DIY-friendly modular design—where every option includes installation difficulty ratings—is a defining commercial strategy. Notably, power windows were dropped from the marketplace, and without the optional $275 telematics module, the phone cannot connect via Bluetooth.

insights.claim: Slate Auto's electric pickup truck starts at $24,950, achieving a sub-$25,000 entry price with a 63 kWh usable LFP battery pack, 181 hp rear-wheel drive motor, 205-mile EPA range estimate, and 120 kW DC fast charging via NACS.
insights.speaker: Jonathan M. Gitlin
insights.so_what: Sub-$25K EV pricing is a market landmark that could pressure Chevy Bolt and entry-level EV segment pricing.
insights.quote: the Blank Slate pickup really does start at just $24,950

insights.claim: A work-truck configuration of the Slate EV can be built for under $30,000 with a structural bed liner, toolbox, locking tailgate, MOLLE webbing, tow hitch (rated up to 2,000 lbs), and telematics module.
insights.speaker: Jonathan M. Gitlin
insights.so_what: Sub-$30K electric work truck targets fleet and tradesperson buyers, a segment with high utility-vehicle replacement volume.
insights.quote: a no-nonsense work truck spec is possible at a sticker price still under $30,000

insights.claim: The Slate Squareback SUV body style starts at $29,950 and the Fastback SUV at $31,950—both add rear bench seats, a roll cage, and extra airbags versus the base pickup, while remaining significantly cheaper than most electric crossovers.
insights.speaker: Jonathan M. Gitlin
insights.so_what: Slate's $30-37K SUV range undercuts mainstream electric crossovers like the Model Y, positioning it as a mass-market disruptor.
insights.quote: The pink SUV was my most expensive build, but it's still far less expensive than most electric crossovers.
insights.locator: This SUV is not for squares section

insights.claim: Upgrading to all-terrain tires from the stock 17-inch steel wheels adds at least $1,000 to the Slate's price, making a fully accessorized off-road variant approach $33,000 before a lift kit that does not yet have a disclosed price.
insights.speaker: Jonathan M. Gitlin
insights.so_what: Optional accessory pricing will materially affect real-world average selling prices; lift kit and specialty-tire demand may lift ASP above advertised base.
insights.quote: upgrading from the stock 17-inch wheels will add at least $1,000 to your Slate

insights.claim: The Slate configurator rates each option by DIY installation difficulty, required tools, and time, reflecting a deliberate strategy to transfer labor cost to the buyer and keep base vehicle prices low.
insights.speaker: Jonathan M. Gitlin
insights.so_what: DIY-installation model reduces Slate's build cost but creates customer-experience risk; how well buyers actually install options will affect brand perception.
insights.quote: a little icon tells you how easy or hard it should be to install that part yourself, what kind of tools you might need, and how long it might take

insights.claim: The option for electric power windows was removed from Slate's marketplace at launch; buyers must use hand-cranked windows, with no commitment from the company that power windows will return.
insights.speaker: Jonathan M. Gitlin
insights.so_what: Feature omissions at launch could alienate mainstream buyers accustomed to basic conveniences, a material risk to achieving broad market adoption.
insights.quote: the ability to add electric window controls, which we believed would be possible, has not made the cut
